assumptions: Structural reorganization of school grades (primary/secondary school span) and increases in special needs identification are treated as analytically separable phenomena that can move on different timelines and for different reasons in the Polish case; no causal linkage between them is assumed unless explicitly stated

Poland's financing mechanisms, gatekeeping structures, and implementation patterns are treated as illustrative comparative reference points, not as predictions for Hungary; direct transferability is not assumed without explicit argument

Rising identification numbers in Poland (particularly for autism) reflect some combination of real prevalence increase, improved diagnostics/awareness, and finance-incentive effects; the source itself does not resolve this mixture, and no single attribution is assumed here

findings.claim: Poland's 2016–2017 structural reorganization reversed the 1999 reform direction by replacing 6-year elementary schools with 8-year primary schools, abolishing lower secondary schools (gymnasiums), and replacing 3-year lower secondary schools with 4-year secondary schools
limitations: Limited to Polish context; direct applicability to Hungarian system design depends on structural similarities not yet established
Does not specify implementation timeline or transition costs in detail

findings.claim: Poland's 2016–2017 reorganization explicitly included rollback of special needs mainstreaming policies, reducing inclusion of students with special needs in mainstream classes
limitations: Sourced from a single secondary synthesis not independently cross-verified in the research round
Lack of quantitative specification of the magnitude of mainstreaming reduction
Unclear whether rollback was reversible or embedded in structural design
findings.source_title: World Class Learning Systems synthesis of Polish education reform

findings.claim: Poland funds special educational needs support through per-pupil disability weightings ranging from 1.4 for mild intellectual disability to 9.5 for profound intellectual disability, with autism diagnoses unlocking subsidies up to approximately 9,500 zł compared to 1,400 zł for mild disability
limitations: Exchange-rate sensitivity affects cross-national comparison
Figures are historical and may not reflect 2025 algorithm changes
Does not separately account for regional variation in funding or diagnostic thresholds
findings.source_title: Polish SEN financing mechanism and weighted subsidies

findings.claim: As of 1 January 2025, Poland replaced its 'subwencja oświatowa' with 'kwota potrzeb oświatowych' algorithm using per-pupil base standard (9,477 zł in 2025) multiplied by disability weights; the subvention for disabled pupils increased 14% from 2023 to 2024 (6,899 zł to 7,866 zł), independent of prevalence changes
limitations: Current and may change with future government; not yet subject to implementation evaluation
Does not specify whether increased per-pupil subvention correlates with diagnostic thresholds or identification behavior
Regional funding distribution rules under the new algorithm not detailed

findings.claim: Diagnostic authority for special education status in Poland is centralized in Psychological and Pedagogical Counselling Centres and education superintendents, not vested in school headmasters or teachers; this creates a gatekeeping structure separate from schools
limitations: Does not quantify capacity constraints or throughput bottlenecks at Counselling and Guidance Centres
Does not address whether centralized gatekeeping reduces or merely displaces diagnostic discretion
Unclear how referral bias from schools to assessment centers affects effective supply of diagnoses

findings.claim: Poland's 2016–2017 structural reform was implemented rapidly against documented objections from educational researchers, local authorities, parents, and teachers, without extended consensus-building
limitations: Implementation speed was politically driven, not necessarily indicative of feasibility or reversibility in other contexts
Does not quantify magnitude of stakeholder opposition or measure counterfactual acceptance under alternative implementation timelines
Polish political economy may not transfer to Hungarian institutional context
findings.source_title: Polish education reform implementation and stakeholder responses

findings.claim: Implementation of Poland's gymnasium phase-out produced a double-cohort crunch in 2017, with both gymnasium graduates and primary school graduates competing for high school places; in Warsaw alone, 3,173 students did not secure high school placement, leading to unplanned technical school enrollment
limitations: Specific to one city (Warsaw) transition; may not reflect system-wide capacity distribution
Does not disaggregate impact by special educational needs status
Does not establish whether capacity constraints were temporary or structural

findings.claim: Poland's reported special educational needs identification for autism increased 30% in a single year (2022/2023 school year to 82,199 children), with regional increases exceeding 1,000% in some provinces, and the male-to-female diagnostic ratio declined from 4.56 (2012) to 2.99 (2023), suggesting improved recognition of autism in females
limitations: The causal attribution to real prevalence increase versus improved diagnostics versus finance incentive effects is explicitly contested in the source itself
Baseline year 2012 figures (18,924) and 2022/23 figures (82,199) may not use identical diagnostic categories, preventing direct prevalence comparison
Regional variation data does not explain underlying drivers (assessment capacity, awareness, funding changes, diagnostic criteria shifts)
findings.source_title: Polish autism and SEN identification trends (2022–2023)

findings.claim: Poland's 2019 teacher strike involving almost 80% of schools was partially motivated by frustration over implementation chaos from the 2016–2017 structural reform, alongside poor working conditions and salaries
limitations: Strike causation was multifactorial; reform-related chaos is one factor among several workplace grievances
Does not quantify the proportion of striker motivation attributable to structural reform versus salary/conditions
Does not measure downstream impact of strike on SEN identification or service delivery
findings.source_title: Polish teacher strikes and education system discontent (2019)

findings.claim: Whether Poland's reversal of 1999–2015 comprehensive education reforms contributed to the large PISA 2022 decline remains contested and unresolved; alternative explanations include persistence of reforms in teacher autonomy and family beliefs about tertiary attainment that were not reversed
limitations: Causality is explicitly framed as an open question in the source, not established
Confounding factors (pandemic effects, test administration changes, international comparison shifts) not separated from reform effects
Multiple reform components were reversed at different times; aggregate effect cannot be attributed to single policy change
findings.source_title: Polish PISA decline and education reform sequencing (speculative)

uncertainties.question: What proportion of Poland's reported 30% increase in autism SEN identification (2022–2023) is attributable to real prevalence change versus improved diagnostic awareness versus activation of finance incentives created by per-pupil disability weighting in the 2025 funding algorithm?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Comparative longitudinal analysis of identification rates in Polish regions with differential funding uptake and Counselling Centre capacity; cross-national diagnostic validation studies using identical criteria (DSM-5 or ICD-11) across cohorts; expert panels reviewing whether diagnostic thresholds or gate-keeping practice shifted contemporaneously with 2025 funding reform

uncertainties.question: Did Poland's Counselling and Guidance Centre capacity constraints limit or accelerate the observed rise in SEN identification between 2012 and 2023, and would expanded capacity increase or decrease the prevalence of recorded diagnoses?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Administrative data analysis of assessment center referral wait-times, rejection rates, and throughput before/after 2025 funding algorithm change; qualitative interviews with diagnostic center directors on demand pressure and diagnostic decision-making; pilot capacity expansion in a subset of regions with measurement of identification rate changes

uncertainties.question: What portion of the implementation crunch in Poland's 2016–2017 structural reform (double cohorts, placement failures) was avoidable through sequencing and capacity planning, and how would the cost of avoiding it compare to the political cost of delaying reform?
uncertainties.reduction_path: Comparative case analysis of other countries' school structural reforms with different implementation timelines; cost-benefit modeling of phased versus rapid implementation with sensitivity analysis on capacity elasticity; expert retrospective assessment of feasibility of alternative sequencing under Polish 2015 political constraints
